Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'hishinuma2006oxyrregulatedthe pages 1-2'.

Sota Hishinuma, Masahiro Yuki, Makoto Fujimura, and Fumiyasu Fukumori. Oxyr regulated the expression of two major catalases, kata and katb, along with peroxiredoxin, ahpc in pseudomonas putida. Environmental microbiology, 8 12:2115-24, Dec 2006. URL: https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1462-2920.2006.01088.x, doi:10.1111/j.1462-2920.2006.01088.x. This article has 86 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports that Pseudomonas putida AhpC is the small subunit of alkyl hydroperoxide reductase (a peroxiredoxin) that has very high affinity ("extremely low Km values") for both organic hydroperoxides and H2O2, indicating substrate specificity for these hydroperoxides. Oxidized AhpC is reactivated by reduction via the dedicated peroxiredoxin reductase AhpF, a reaction that requires NADH. AhpC expression is part of the OxyR-controlled oxidative stress response (ahpC is in the oxyR regulon/ahpCF context) and is upregulated by oxidative stress; a described oxyR1 mutation increased ahpC transcription and caused accumulation of AhpC (and KatA) throughout growth. Purified OxyR binds upstream sequences of ahpC under both reduced and oxidized states, consistent with direct transcriptional regulation. The authors note a physiological limitation: because AhpC reduction depends on AhpF and NADH, AhpC "may not be suitable for detoxifying a large quantity of peroxides." The excerpt does not provide specific kinetic constants (numeric Km), cellular localization, exact operon map or precise fold-change values for ahpC induction, only qualitative/relative statements and catalase activity data for other enzymes.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'fukumori2001molecularcloningand pages 6-8'.

Fumiyasu Fukumori and Mitsuru Kishii. Molecular cloning and transcriptional analysis of the alkyl hydroperoxide reductase genes from pseudomonas putida kt2442. The Journal of general and applied microbiology, 47 5:269-277, Oct 2001. URL: https://doi.org/10.2323/jgam.47.269, doi:10.2323/jgam.47.269. This article has 21 citations.

The excerpt reports that AhpC (alkyl hydroperoxide reductase subunit C) reduces alkyl hydroperoxide substrates to the corresponding alcohol and that oxidized AhpC is regenerated by AhpF. It states that hydrogen peroxide may not be a preferred substrate for P. putida AHPR. Functionally, overproduction of AhpC in the toluene-resistant variant KT2442TOL correlates with increased resistance to BHP, whereas both KT2442TOL and KT2442 show similar sensitivity to hydrogen peroxide. Heterologous expression data indicate P. putida ahpC partially protected E. coli DH5a from killing by BHP but not by hydrogen peroxide, while overproduction of P. putida AhpF in E. coli increased sensitivity to oxidants. Genetically, ahpC and ahpF are co-transcribed but ahpF is expressed at much lower levels, likely due to transcriptional down-regulation by stem-and-loop structures between the genes. Regulation is OxyR-dependent: an oxyR1 point mutation (T to A at position 316 causing Phe106→Ile) constitutively increases AHPR expression (suggesting OxyR1 is locked in an oxidized/active form), and an engineered KT2442TOL-oxyR strain did not overexpress AhpC. Quantitative data in the excerpt include colony-formation under toluene for KT2442TOL-oxyR (~51 ± 8%) versus KT2442TOL (~92 ± 10%). The excerpt does not state cellular localization, detailed kinetics, or specific fold-change/activity numbers beyond the colony percentages and qualitative resistance/susceptibility observations.


---

## pqac-00000002

From text 'fukumori2001molecularcloningand pages 4-6'.

Fumiyasu Fukumori and Mitsuru Kishii. Molecular cloning and transcriptional analysis of the alkyl hydroperoxide reductase genes from pseudomonas putida kt2442. The Journal of general and applied microbiology, 47 5:269-277, Oct 2001. URL: https://doi.org/10.2323/jgam.47.269, doi:10.2323/jgam.47.269. This article has 21 citations.

The P. putida 24-kDa soluble protein is an AhpC homologue (peroxiredoxin): AhpC is 186 amino acids (~20,376 Da) with conserved cysteines at positions 47 and 166. Downstream ahpF likely encodes a 520-aa flavoprotein (~55,349 Da) that "possesses conserved FAD binding domains (212 to 227 and 468 to 486) and NAD(P)H binding domain (355 to 369)", implying electron transfer from NAD(P)H via AhpF to AhpC. The protein is inducibly produced when cultures are supplemented with hydrogen peroxide or BHP (toluene does not induce it). ahpC and ahpF are co-transcribed (primer extension mapping: main start at C position 269) and show signs of operon-like organization; a stem-loop in the ahpC–ahpF intergenic region causes transcriptional attenuation resulting in lower AhpF accumulation, and deletion of that stem-loop increases ahpF expression. An OxyR-like binding motif (ATAG-like) is found upstream of the transcription start, and "Point mutation of oxyR caused the overexpression of ahpC", indicating OxyR regulation. Functional phenotypes reported: overexpression of ahpC increases resistance to BHP but not to H2O2; overexpression of ahpF alone increases sensitivity to H2O2 and BHP; co-overexpression (ahpC+ahpF) yields resistance to BHP but sensitivity to H2O2. Northern blot sizes: ahpF mRNAs up to ~2.7 kb; ahpC major species ~0.7 kb. The excerpt contains protein sizes and expression/phenotype data but no kinetic parameters, specific enzymatic rate constants, or fold-change quantitation.

## pqac-00000003

From text 'fukumori2001molecularcloningand pages 1-3'.

Fumiyasu Fukumori and Mitsuru Kishii. Molecular cloning and transcriptional analysis of the alkyl hydroperoxide reductase genes from pseudomonas putida kt2442. The Journal of general and applied microbiology, 47 5:269-277, Oct 2001. URL: https://doi.org/10.2323/jgam.47.269, doi:10.2323/jgam.47.269. This article has 21 citations.

The excerpt reports cloning and characterization of Pseudomonas putida ahpC (24-kDa soluble protein; peroxiredoxin/AhpC) and the adjacent ahpF (large subunit). ahpC and ahpF are colocated and RNA analyses indicate they are co-transcribed; lower ahpF levels may result from transcriptional attenuation via stem-loop structures between the genes. Functionally, the two-component AHPR system reduces and detoxifies organic hydroperoxides and hydrogen peroxide. AhpF transfers electrons from NADH to AhpC, which then reduces the peroxide substrate. Expression of ahpC is regulated by OxyR: a point mutation (Phe106→Ile) in OxyR in the toluene-resistant KT2442TOL strain causes constitutive overexpression of AhpC; allelic reversal of oxyR abolishes overexpression and about 50% of reverse-mutant cells lose colony-forming ability in toluene, linking oxyR mutation, ahp overexpression, and solvent resistance (partial effect). Methods for assessing oxidative sensitivity included disk diffusion assays with H2O2 and t‑butyl hydroperoxide. The excerpt does not provide enzymatic kinetics, specific substrate KM/Vmax values, quantitative fold-changes of ahp expression, or direct cellular compartmentation beyond being a soluble protein.

## pqac-00000004

From text 'kim2014oxidativestressresponse pages 5-6'.

Jisun Kim and Woojun Park. Oxidative stress response in pseudomonas putida. Applied Microbiology and Biotechnology, 98:6933-6946, Jun 2014. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s00253-014-5883-4, doi:10.1007/s00253-014-5883-4. This article has 142 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports that Pseudomonas putida AhpC is a peroxiredoxin that detoxifies hydroperoxides (including low levels of hydrogen peroxide). Oxidized AhpC is reactivated by the dedicated peroxiredoxin reductase AhpF, a homodimeric flavoenzyme/disulfide reductase that uses NADH as the reducing equivalent; because AhpC/AhpF depend on reducing equivalents they may be inadequate for detoxifying large peroxide loads. AhpC expression is controlled by OxyR (co-regulated with catalases KatA and KatB) and is induced by oxidative stresses such as superoxide exposure and by chemical pollutants (methyl tert-butyl ether, phenol, benzoate). In KT2442 ahpC (and katA/katB) transcripts are low in exponential phase and increase in early stationary phase. A 2-Cys peroxiredoxin-like protein in KT2440 shows dual peroxidase and chaperone activity under oxidative stress. The excerpt does not provide quantitative fold-changes for ahpC expression, specific kinetic parameters, enzymatic rates, or explicit cellular localization for AhpC/AhpF.

## pqac-00000005

From text 'fukumori2001molecularcloningand pages 3-4'.

Fumiyasu Fukumori and Mitsuru Kishii. Molecular cloning and transcriptional analysis of the alkyl hydroperoxide reductase genes from pseudomonas putida kt2442. The Journal of general and applied microbiology, 47 5:269-277, Oct 2001. URL: https://doi.org/10.2323/jgam.47.269, doi:10.2323/jgam.47.269. This article has 21 citations.

The excerpt reports cloning and partial biochemical characterization of Pseudomonas putida ahpC and linked ahpF on a 2,773-bp SalI–SmaI fragment (GenBank AB010689). The predicted ORF is highly homologous to S. typhimurium AhpC, leading the authors to identify it as an AhpC homologue. AhpC was recovered in the soluble fraction and eluted at ~0.2 M from DEAE-Sepharose, and an abundant soluble protein of ~24 kDa was overexpressed in the toluene-adapted KT2442TOL strain. In the parental strain this 24-kDa protein was inducibly produced by supplementation with hydrogen peroxide or t-butyl hydroperoxide (BHP), but not by toluene. The paper used the coding regions of ahpC (positions 387–866) and ahpF (1623–2269) for probes, showing ahpC and ahpF are adjacent. Phenotypic data (Table 2) give zones of inhibition (mm) for oxidants: KT2442 (H2O2 23 ±1; BHP 30 ±2), KT2442TOL (H2O2 24 ±1; BHP 14 ±1), and KT2442TOL-oxyR (H2O2 25 ±1; BHP 30 ±2), indicating altered sensitivity/resistance related to the TOL-adapted strain and oxyR. The excerpt does not present explicit enzymatic reaction equations, kinetic parameters, or a demonstrated electron donor mechanism, though the presence of ahpF adjacent to ahpC is noted.

## pqac-00000006

From text 'dubbs2007peroxiredoxinsinbacterial pages 9-12'.

James M. Dubbs and Skorn Mongkolsuk. Peroxiredoxins in bacterial antioxidant defense. Sub-cellular biochemistry, 44:143-93, Jan 2007. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-4020-6051-9\_7, doi:10.1007/978-1-4020-6051-9\_7. This article has 133 citations.

For Pseudomonas putida KT2442TOL, the ahpC-ahpF operon produces a predominant ~700 bp ahpC transcript and a less abundant ~2700 nt transcript encoding ahpC and ahpF, initiating 37 nt upstream of the ahpC start. The upstream region contains a sequence similar to the E. coli OxyR binding motif, and an additional ~1400 nt ahpF mRNA has been detected (possibly from processing or a second promoter). Differential accumulation of ahpC versus ahpCF transcripts corresponds to relative protein levels in crude extracts. In Pseudomonas more broadly, OxyR binds near the ahpC promoter and is required for ahpC promoter activity (inactivation of OxyR abolishes activity); ahpCF expression is induced by H2O2 and cumene hydroperoxide. Comparative quantitative data in the excerpt: E. coli ahpC/ahpF are induced ~20-fold by H2O2 (OxyR-dependent), and Xanthomonas AhpC synthesis increases 4–5-fold in response to peroxides, organic superoxide inducers (menadione, paraquat) and CdCl2. The excerpt does not provide explicit enzymatic reaction details, substrate specificity, reduction mechanism (AhpF vs thioredoxin), cellular localization, or kinetic constants for P. putida AhpC.

## pqac-00000007

From text 'hishinuma2006oxyrregulatedthe pages 6-7'.

Sota Hishinuma, Masahiro Yuki, Makoto Fujimura, and Fumiyasu Fukumori. Oxyr regulated the expression of two major catalases, kata and katb, along with peroxiredoxin, ahpc in pseudomonas putida. Environmental microbiology, 8 12:2115-24, Dec 2006. URL: https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1462-2920.2006.01088.x, doi:10.1111/j.1462-2920.2006.01088.x. This article has 86 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports transcriptional and regulatory features of Pseudomonas putida ahpC (peroxiredoxin). ahpC transcription initiates from two closely spaced start sites (designated P1 at −37 and P2 at −28), separated by nine bases, and these transcripts are present without peroxide challenge and in an rpoS mutant (so ahpC expression is RpoS-independent). Gel-shift data indicate the reduced form of OxyR binds the ahpC promoter in the absence of peroxide, whereas only oxidized OxyR can activate transcription by contacting the RNA polymerase α subunit. The oxyR1 mutation causes large upregulation of ahpC transcript levels: real-time PCR threshold cycles for ahpC are reported as LOG oxyR = 20.10 ± 0.34 versus LOG oxyR1 = 12.49 ± 0.75, and ST oxyR = 15.16 ± 1.43 versus ST oxyR1 = 10.67 ± 0.15 (Table 1). The authors note decreases of ~5–6 Ct between growth phases correspond to ~30–60-fold transcript increases, and the oxyR1-related differences (e.g., ~7.6 Ct in log phase) imply very large fold-changes. The excerpt does not provide enzymatic reaction, substrate specificity, reducing partner (AhpF or thioredoxin), cellular localization, detailed activities or kinetics, nor direct phenotypic assays under chemical/oxidative stress beyond transcript-level changes.

Abstract:
Nanoparticles play a crucial role in the field of nanotechnology, offering different properties due to their surface area attributed to their small size. Among them, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have attracted significant attention due to their antimicrobial properties, with applications that date back from ancient medicinal practices to contemporary commercial products containing ions or silver nanoparticles. AgNPs possess broad-spectrum biocidal potential against bacteria, fungi, viruses, and Mycobacterium, in addition to exhibiting synergistic effects when combined with certain antibiotics. The mechanisms underlying its antimicrobial action include the generation of oxygen-reactive species, damage to DNA, rupture of bacterial cell membranes and inhibition of protein synthesis. Recent studies have highlighted the effectiveness of AgNPs against various clinically relevant bacterial strains through their potential to combat antibiotic-resistant pathogens. This review investigates the proteomic mechanisms by which AgNPs exert their antimicrobial effects, with a special focus on their activity against planktonic bacteria and in biofilms. Furthermore, it discusses the biomedical applications of AgNPs and their potential non-preparation of antibiotic formulations, also addressing the issue of resistance to antibiotics.

## pqac-00000008

From text 'rodriguezcastro2024thelongchainflavodoxin pages 10-11'.

Laura Rodríguez-Castro, Roberto E. Durán, Valentina Méndez, Flavia Dorochesi, Daniela Zühlke, Katharina Riedel, and Michael Seeger. The long-chain flavodoxin fldx1 improves the biodegradation of 4-hydroxyphenylacetate and 3-hydroxyphenylacetate and counteracts the oxidative stress associated to aromatic catabolism in paraburkholderia xenovorans. Biological Research, Apr 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1186/s40659-024-00491-4, doi:10.1186/s40659-024-00491-4. This article has 6 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt (Rodríguez‑Castro et al., 2024) analyzed antioxidant genes in Paraburkholderia xenovorans LB400 during growth on 3‑ and 4‑hydroxyphenylacetate (HPAs). Alkyl hydroperoxide reductase (ahpC1) was among the ROS‑scavenging genes selected for qRT‑PCR/proteomic comparison alongside katE, sodB1, oxyR, trxB1/2, ohrB and fumC. The authors report that, overall, no >2‑fold changes in gene expression were observed versus glucose, but specific redox proteins showed condition‑dependent changes: several thiol‑redox proteins (GstB3, TrxA3) and an AcnA2 isoenzyme were upregulated in WT‑p2 on 4‑HPA, while multiple peroxiredoxins and Hpf were downregulated. Overexpression of the long‑chain flavodoxin FldX1 (strain p2‑fldX1) produced a slight repression tendency for stress genes (examples listed include katE, sodB1, trxB1, ohrB and fumC), leading the authors to suggest FldX1 provides a protective redox effect that reduces the need for antioxidant induction. Functionally, p2‑fldX1 improved bioremediation metrics in soil microcosms: increased numbers of 4‑HPA degraders by day 14, faster 4‑HPA removal at days 3 and 7, complete degradation by the end of the assay, and somewhat better plasmid retention (noting 10–30% plasmid remained at assay end). Thus, while direct quantitative fold‑change values for ahpC1 are not clearly provided in this excerpt, the study links alkyl hydroperoxide reductase/other antioxidant systems to aromatic‑compound stress responses and demonstrates an applied bioremediation benefit when oxidative stress is mitigated by FldX1 overexpression.

From text 'bojanovic2017globaltranscriptionalresponses pages 10-11'.

Klara Bojanovič, Isotta D'Arrigo, and Katherine S. Long. Global transcriptional responses to osmotic, oxidative, and imipenem stress conditions in pseudomonas putida. Applied and Environmental Microbiology, Apr 2017. URL: https://doi.org/10.1128/aem.03236-16, doi:10.1128/aem.03236-16. This article has 82 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This Pseudomonas putida KT2440 transcriptomic dataset (H2O2 exposure) shows strong, rapid induction of peroxiredoxin-related genes: ahpC (PP_2439) increased ~74.7-fold at T1 (7 min) and ~2.3-fold at T2 (60 min); ahpF (PP_2440) increased ~203.4-fold at T1 and ~3.5-fold at T2; PP_2441 showed ~247.3-fold at T1 and ~4.4-fold at T2. The response to inorganic hydrogen peroxide is much greater at 7 min than at 60 min, with 1,746 (32.6% CDS) and 814 (15.2% CDS) differentially expressed genes at T1 and T2, respectively. Catalase katA (PP_0481) is also massively induced (936.5-fold at T1), highlighting coordinated ROS detoxification involving catalases and peroxiredoxins. The excerpt frames peroxiredoxins as part of canonical ROS-defense enzymes and places these transcriptional changes within known oxidative stress regulatory contexts (OxyR/SoxR differences across species). Timepoints are defined as T1 = 7 min and T2 = 60 min. These are transcript-level fold-change data from the 2017 study and provide baseline quantitative expression evidence for AhpC/AhpF in P. putida KT2440 under H2O2 stress.

Table 2 on page 10 reports the fold-change values for *ahpC* (PP_2439) and *ahpF* (PP_2440) under H2O2 stress at two time points: T1 (7 min) and T2 (60 min). 

Under H2O2 stress:
- **ahpC (PP_2439)**: fold change of 74.7 at T1 and 2.3 at T2.
- **ahpF (PP_2440)**: fold change of 203.4 at T1 and 3.5 at T2.

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a markdown table summarizing functional annotation for Pseudomonas putida KT2440/KT2442 AhpC (PP_2439; UniProt Q88K52): columns should include Aspect (identity/domains, reaction/substrates, electron donor/regeneration, regulation, operon/transcription, localization, physiological roles/phenotypes, quantitative expression stats), Key findings (concise), Evidence type (primary study/review), and Citations (pqac IDs). Include quantitative numbers like inhibition zones, fold-changes, Ct values when available."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Aspect | Key findings | Evidence type | Citations |
|---|---|---|---|
| Identity/domains | Target identity is consistent across sources: **ahpC = PP_2439** in *Pseudomonas putida* KT2440/KT2442, encoding a **small, soluble ~24 kDa alkyl hydroperoxide reductase subunit C/peroxiredoxin**. The protein is a **2-Cys peroxiredoxin** with conserved cysteines at positions **Cys47** and **Cys166**, matching the AhpC/Prx1 family assignment. | Primary study; review | (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000009) |
| Reaction/substrates | AhpC is the **peroxidatic subunit** of alkyl hydroperoxide reductase and reduces **organic hydroperoxides to the corresponding alcohols**; it can also detoxify **H2O2**, but evidence in *P. putida* suggests **organic hydroperoxides are preferred substrates** and H2O2 may be less preferred. Reviews note very low Km values for hydroperoxides qualitatively, but no numeric kinetics were recovered here for *P. putida* AhpC. | Primary study; review | (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000004) |
| Electron donor/regeneration | Oxidized AhpC is regenerated by **AhpF**, the dedicated **NADH-dependent flavo-disulfide reductase/peroxiredoxin reductase**. AhpF contains conserved **FAD-binding** and **NAD(P)H-binding** motifs, supporting electron flow from **NADH → AhpF → AhpC → peroxide substrate**. | Primary study; review | (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004) |
| Regulation | **OxyR directly regulates ahpC**. Purified OxyR binds upstream of ahpC; reduced OxyR can bind promoter DNA, whereas oxidized OxyR is linked to transcriptional activation. An **oxyR1** mutation causes constitutively elevated ahpC expression. | Primary study; review | (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007) |
| Operon/transcription | **ahpC and ahpF are co-transcribed** in an **ahpCF operon**. A major **~0.7 kb ahpC transcript** and a less abundant **~2.7 kb ahpCF transcript** were detected; an additional **~1.4 kb ahpF RNA** may arise from processing or a second promoter. A stem-loop between ahpC and ahpF likely attenuates downstream ahpF expression, explaining lower AhpF abundance. Transcription starts were mapped to two ahpC starts (**P1 = -37, P2 = -28**). | Primary study; review | (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007) |
| Localization | Experimental descriptions identify AhpC as a **soluble protein** recovered in the **soluble/cytosolic fraction**; no evidence here supports secretion or membrane localization. Function is therefore most parsimoniously assigned to the **cytosol**, where it detoxifies intracellular peroxides generated by metabolism or chemical stress. | Primary study; inference from fractionation/review | (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000005) |
| Physiological roles/phenotypes | AhpC contributes to **oxidative stress defense**, especially against **organic hydroperoxides**. In KT2442, induction occurred with **H2O2** or **t-butyl hydroperoxide (BHP)**, but not toluene alone. Overproduction of AhpC in the toluene-adapted strain correlated with improved BHP resistance: inhibition zones were **30 ± 2 mm** in KT2442, **14 ± 1 mm** in KT2442TOL, and **30 ± 2 mm** in the oxyR-revertant; H2O2 zones remained similar (**23 ± 1**, **24 ± 1**, **25 ± 1 mm**, respectively). Heterologous expression of *P. putida* ahpC partially protected *E. coli* against BHP but not H2O2. Reversion of oxyR reduced colony formation in toluene from **~92 ± 10%** in KT2442TOL to **~51 ± 8%** in KT2442TOL-oxyR, linking OxyR/AhpC overexpression with solvent-associated stress tolerance. | Primary study; review | (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000004) |
| Quantitative expression stats | Under H2O2 stress in KT2440, **ahpC (PP_2439)** increased **74.7-fold at 7 min** and **2.3-fold at 60 min**; **ahpF (PP_2440)** increased **203.4-fold at 7 min** and **3.5-fold at 60 min**. In an oxyR1 background, ahpC qPCR Ct values were **20.10 ± 0.34** (LOG oxyR) vs **12.49 ± 0.75** (LOG oxyR1), and **15.16 ± 1.43** (ST oxyR) vs **10.67 ± 0.15** (ST oxyR1), indicating very large transcript increases in oxyR1. | Primary study | (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010) |


*Table: This table summarizes the core functional annotation for *Pseudomonas putida* AhpC (PP_2439; UniProt Q88K52), including enzymatic role, regulation, operon structure, localization, phenotypes, and quantitative expression evidence. It is useful as a compact evidence map linking the curated protein identity to experimental studies in *P. putida*.*

function.arguments: {"plan_objective_id": 4, "updated_plan_objective_json": "{\"id\":4,\"objective\":\"Identify current applications/implementations and quantitative statistics (e.g., expression changes, stress survival phenotypes, kinetic parameters, redox potentials) from recent studies; include expert opinions from reviews.\",\"rationale\":\"Meets user request for real-world implementations, expert analysis, and relevant data.\",\"status\":\"completed\",\"result\":\"Compiled quantitative stress-response statistics (2017 transcriptome fold-changes; 2001 inhibition-zone phenotypes and colony formation under toluene) and incorporated applied implementations from 2024 environmental biodegradation/bioremediation literature where antioxidant systems including AhpC participate. Expert analysis from reviews (2007, 2014) included regarding limitations of AhpCF at high peroxide loads and integration into OxyR regulon.\",\"evaluation\":\"Objective met with multiple quantitative datapoints and real-world implementation examples; remaining limitation is scarcity of P. putida KT2440-specific 2023\u20132024 primary studies centered on AhpC biochemistry within the retrieved corpus.\"}"}
function.name: update_plan

messages.content:
Updated plan:
{
  "objectives": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "objective": "Verify target identity for UniProt Q88K52 (ahpC) in Pseudomonas putida KT2440 and collect authoritative database annotations (UniProt/InterPro/KEGG/GO where possible).",
      "rationale": "Ensures the report addresses the correct gene/protein and establishes baseline function, domains, and localization from curated resources.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Identity cross-check: P. putida KT2440 ahpC is locus PP_2439 (Table 2 in Bojanovič et al. 2017), matching user-provided UniProt ordered locus name PP_2439 and description as alkyl hydroperoxide reductase C/peroxiredoxin (AhpC/Prx1 subfamily). Literature reviewed describes AhpC as soluble 2-Cys peroxiredoxin/peroxidase reduced by AhpF (NADH-dependent).",
      "evaluation": "Confirms the correct gene/protein (ahpC; PP_2439) in the correct organism/strain context and aligns with peroxiredoxin family assignment."
    },
    {
      "id": 2,
      "objective": "Retrieve and read primary literature and recent reviews (prioritize 2023–2024) relevant to AhpC/alkyl hydroperoxide reductase C/peroxiredoxin function in Pseudomonas putida or closely related Pseudomonas, including enzymatic mechanism, substrates, and pathway context (AhpF/thioredoxin).",
      "rationale": "Provides experimentally supported functional annotation and current understanding; recent work may include stress response, redox signaling, and industrial/biotech contexts.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Collected and synthesized foundational P. putida primary literature (2001, 2006) plus authoritative reviews (2007, 2014) defining AhpC reaction (hydroperoxide reduction), coupling to AhpF/NADH, and OxyR control; additionally incorporated applied/updated context from 2024 oxidative-stress/bioremediation work in a related environmental betaproteobacterium (Paraburkholderia xenovorans).",
      "evaluation": "Meets core functional-mechanistic requirements; however, truly P. putida-specific 2023–2024 primary literature with detailed AhpC biochemistry was limited in retrieved corpus."
    },
    {
      "id": 3,
      "objective": "Gather evidence on cellular localization, operon context (ahpCF), regulation (OxyR/PerR-like), and physiological roles (oxidative stress, organic peroxide detoxification, biofilm, virulence, solvent tolerance).",
      "rationale": "Addresses where the gene product acts and its role in pathways and cellular processes.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Evidence assembled for: (i) cytosolic/soluble localization from fractionation; (ii) ahpCF operon co-transcription with attenuation via stem-loop and mapped transcription start sites; (iii) direct OxyR binding/activation model and oxyR1 constitutive phenotype; (iv) physiological roles in organic peroxide detoxification and stress tolerance including solvent-associated phenotypes (toluene-adapted strain) and rapid oxidative-stress induction under H2O2.",
      "evaluation": "Objective met with multiple independent primary sources and an authoritative review linking operon/regulation to phenotypes."
    },
    {
      "id": 4,
      "objective": "Identify current applications/implementations and quantitative statistics (e.g., expression changes, stress survival phenotypes, kinetic parameters, redox potentials) from recent studies; include expert opinions from reviews.",
      "rationale": "Meets user request for real-world implementations, expert analysis, and relevant data.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Compiled quantitative stress-response statistics (2017 transcriptome fold-changes; 2001 inhibition-zone phenotypes and colony formation under toluene) and incorporated applied implementations from 2024 environmental biodegradation/bioremediation literature where antioxidant systems including AhpC participate. Expert analysis from reviews (2007, 2014) included regarding limitations of AhpCF at high peroxide loads and integration into OxyR regulon.",
      "evaluation": "Objective met with multiple quantitative datapoints and real-world implementation examples; remaining limitation is scarcity of P. putida KT2440-specific 2023–2024 primary studies centered on AhpC biochemistry within the retrieved corpus."
    },
    {
      "id": 5,
      "objective": "Extract at least one relevant figure/table image from a key paper (e.g., mechanism schematic, kinetic table, or operon/regulation figure) using view_images for visual evidence.",
      "rationale": "Improves evidence quality and complies with tool guidance to include at least one image when feasible.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Retrieved cropped image of Table 2 from Bojanovič et al. 2017 showing H2O2-induced fold changes for ahpC (PP_2439) and ahpF (PP_2440) at 7 min and 60 min timepoints.",
      "evaluation": "Acceptance criteria met: at least one relevant figure/table image was retrieved and can be cited in the final report."
    },
    {
      "id": 6,
      "objective": "Synthesize a comprehensive, well-cited research report with URLs and publication dates, clearly separating concepts/definitions, latest research, applications, expert analysis, and statistics; explicitly note any gaps specific to P. putida Q88K52 if literature is limited.",
      "rationale": "Delivers the final user-facing product with traceable citations and compliance with formatting requirements.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    }
  ]
}

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
query.title: Query

min_year.description:
filter for minimum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
min_year.title: Min Year

max_year.description:
filter for maximum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
max_year.title: Max Year

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility
        - OTHER: Other

    InterventionType:
        - BEHAVIORAL: Behavioral interventions
        - BIOLOGICAL: Biological interventions
        - COMBINATION_PRODUCT: Combination product interventions
        - DEVICE: Device interventions
        - DIAGNOSTIC_TEST: Diagnostic test interventions
        - DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: Dietary supplement interventions
        - DRUG: Drug interventions
        - GENETIC: Genetic interventions
        - PROCEDURE: Procedure interventions
        - RADIATION: Radiation interventions
        - OTHER: Other interventions

    DesignAllocation:
        - RANDOMIZED: Randomized allocation
        - NON_RANDOMIZED: Non-randomized allocation
        - NA: Not applicable

    InterventionalAssignment:
        - SINGLE_GROUP: Single group assignment
        - PARALLEL: Parallel assignment
        - CROSSOVER: Crossover assignment
        - FACTORIAL: Factorial assignment
        - SEQUENTIAL: Sequential assignment

    ObservationalModel:
        - COHORT: Cohort
        - CASE_CONTROL: Case-Control
        - CASE_ONLY: Case-Only
        - CASE_CROSSOVER: Case-Crossover
        - ECOLOGIC_OR_COMMUNITY: Ecologic or Community
        - FAMILY_BASED: Family-Based
        - DEFINED_POPULATION: Defined Population
        - NATURAL_HISTORY: Natural History
        - OTHER: Other

    DesignMasking:
        - NONE: None (Open Label)
        - SINGLE: Single
        - DOUBLE: Double
        - TRIPLE: Triple
        - QUADRUPLE: Quadruple

    WhoMasked:
        - PARTICIPANT: Participant
        - CARE_PROVIDER: Care Provider
        - INVESTIGATOR: Investigator
        - OUTCOMES_ASSESSOR: Outcomes Assessor
query.title: Query

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: You are an expert researcher providing comprehensive, well-cited information.

Provide detailed information focusing on:
1. Key concepts and definitions with current understanding
2. Recent developments and latest research (prioritize 2023-2024 sources)
3. Current applications and real-world implementations
4. Expert opinions and analysis from authoritative sources
5. Relevant statistics and data from recent studies

Format as a comprehensive research report with proper citations. Include URLs and publication dates where available.
Always prioritize recent, authoritative sources and provide specific citations for all major claims.

# Gene Research for Functional Annotation

## ⚠️ CRITICAL: Gene/Protein Identification Context

**BEFORE YOU BEGIN RESEARCH:** You MUST verify you are researching the CORRECT gene/protein. Gene symbols can be ambiguous, especially for less well-characterized genes from non-model organisms.

### Target Gene/Protein Identity (from UniProt):
- **UniProt Accession:** Q88K52
- **Protein Description:** RecName: Full=Alkyl hydroperoxide reductase C {ECO:0000256|ARBA:ARBA00017462, ECO:0000256|RuleBase:RU366004}; EC=1.11.1.26 {ECO:0000256|ARBA:ARBA00013021, ECO:0000256|RuleBase:RU366004}; AltName: Full=Peroxiredoxin {ECO:0000256|ARBA:ARBA00032077, ECO:0000256|RuleBase:RU366004}; AltName: Full=Thioredoxin peroxidase {ECO:0000256|ARBA:ARBA00032824, ECO:0000256|RuleBase:RU366004};
- **Gene Information:** Name=ahpC {ECO:0000313|EMBL:AAN68051.1}; OrderedLocusNames=PP_2439 {ECO:0000313|EMBL:AAN68051.1};
- **Organism (full):** Pseudomonas putida (strain ATCC 47054 / DSM 6125 / CFBP 8728 / NCIMB 11950 / KT2440).
- **Protein Family:** Belongs to the peroxiredoxin family. AhpC/Prx1 subfamily.
- **Key Domains:** AhpC. (IPR017559); AhpC/TSA. (IPR000866); Peroxiredoxin. (IPR050217); Peroxiredoxin_AhpC-typ. (IPR024706); Peroxiredoxin_C. (IPR019479)

### MANDATORY VERIFICATION STEPS:

1. **Check if the gene symbol "ahpC" matches the protein description above**
2. **Verify the organism is correct:** Pseudomonas putida (strain ATCC 47054 / DSM 6125 / CFBP 8728 / NCIMB 11950 / KT2440).
3. **Check if protein family/domains align with what you find in literature**
4. **If you find literature for a DIFFERENT gene with the same or similar symbol, STOP**

### If Gene Symbol is Ambiguous or You Cannot Find Relevant Literature:

**DO NOT PROCEED WITH RESEARCH ON A DIFFERENT GENE.** Instead:
- State clearly: "The gene symbol 'ahpC' is ambiguous or literature is limited for this specific protein"
- Explain what you found (e.g., "Found extensive literature on a different gene with the same symbol in a different organism")
- Describe the protein based ONLY on the UniProt information provided above
- Suggest that the protein function can be inferred from domain/family information

### Research Target:

Please provide a comprehensive research report on the gene **ahpC** (gene ID: ahpC, UniProt: Q88K52) in PSEPK.

The research report should be a detailed narrative explaining the function, biological processes, and localization of the gene product. Citations should be given for all claims.

You should prioritize authoritative reviews and primary scientific literature when conducting research. You can supplement
this with annotations you find in gene/protein databases, but these can be outdated or inaccurate.

We are specifically interested in the primary function of the gene - for enzymes, what reaction is catalyzed, and what is the substrate specificity? For transporters, what is the substrate? For structural proteins or adapters, what is the broader structural role? For signaling molecules, what is the role in the pathway.

We are interested in where in or outside the cell the gene product carries out its function.

We are also interested in the signaling or biochemical pathways in which the gene functions. We are less interested in broad pleiotropic effects, except where these elucidate the precise role.

Include evidence where possible. We are interested in both experimental evidence as well as inference from structure, evolution, or bioinformatic analysis. Precise studies should be prioritized over high-throughput, where available.

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.

Retrieved Papers:
## 1. The OxyR and SoxR transcriptional regulators are involved in a broad oxidative stress response in Paraburkholderia xenovorans LB400
Valid Text Names:
'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 1-2', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 2-4', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 4-6', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 6-7', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 7-10', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 10-12', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 12-13', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 13-15', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 15-16', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 16-17', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 17-18', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 18-19', 'mendez2022theoxyrand pages 19-19'

BibTex:
@article{mendez2022theoxyrand,
    author = "Méndez, Valentina and Rodríguez-Castro, Laura and Durán, Roberto E. and Padrón, Gabriel and Seeger, Michael",
    title = "The OxyR and SoxR transcriptional regulators are involved in a broad oxidative stress response in Paraburkholderia xenovorans LB400",
    year = "2022",
    journal = "Biological Research",
    volume = "55",
    month = "Feb",
    doi = "10.1186/s40659-022-00373-7",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1186/s40659-022-00373-7",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "0717-6287"
}


Abstract:
Background Aerobic metabolism generates reactive oxygen species that may cause critical harm to the cell. The aim of this study is the characterization of the stress responses in the model aromatic-degrading bacterium Paraburkholderia xenovorans LB400 to the oxidizing agents paraquat and H 2 O 2 . Methods Antioxidant genes were identified by bioinformatic methods in the genome of P. xenovorans LB400, and the phylogeny of its OxyR and SoxR transcriptional regulators were studied. Functionality of the transcriptional regulators from strain LB400 was assessed by complementation with LB400 SoxR of null mutant P. aeruginosa ΔsoxR, and the construction of P. xenovorans pIZ oxyR that overexpresses OxyR. The effects of oxidizing agents on P. xenovorans were studied measuring bacterial susceptibility, survival and ROS formation after exposure to paraquat and H 2 O 2 . The effects of these oxidants on gene expression (qRT-PCR) and the proteome (LC–MS/MS) were quantified. Results P. xenovorans LB400 possesses a wide repertoire of genes for the antioxidant defense including the oxyR , ahpC , ahpF , kat , trxB , dpsA and gorA genes, whose orthologous genes are regulated by the transcriptional regulator OxyR in E. coli . The LB400 genome also harbors the soxR, fumC , acnA , sodB , fpr and fldX genes, whose orthologous genes are regulated by the transcriptional regulator SoxR in E. coli . The functionality of the LB400 soxR gene was confirmed by complementation of null mutant P. aeruginosa Δ soxR . Growth, susceptibility, and ROS formation assays revealed that LB400 cells were more susceptible to paraquat than H 2 O 2 . Transcriptional analyses indicated the upregulation of the oxyR , ahpC1 , katE and ohrB genes in LB400 cells after exposure to H 2 O 2 , whereas the oxyR , fumC , ahpC1 , sodB1 and ohrB genes were induced in presence of paraquat. Proteome analysis revealed that paraquat induced the oxidative stress response proteins AhpCF and DpsA, the universal stress protein UspA and the RNA chaperone CspA. Both oxidizing agents induced the Ohr protein, which is involved in organic peroxide resistance. Notably, the overexpression of the LB400 oxyR gene in P. xenovorans significantly decreased the ROS formation and the susceptibility to paraquat, suggesting a broad OxyR-regulated antioxidant response. Conclusions This study showed that P. xenovorans LB400 possess a broad range oxidative stress response, which explain the high resistance of this strain to the oxidizing compounds paraquat and H 2 O 2 .

Abstract:
Background: The oxyR and recG genes appeared to be located within the same operon in many bacteria. Results: OxyR and RecG might work together to control the expression of oxidative stress-related genes. Conclusion: RecG might be required for the induction of the OxyR regulon by unwinding palindromic DNA for transcription. Significance: This provides novel insights into the oxidative stress transcriptional machinery in Pseudomonas. The oxyR gene appears to reside in an operon with the recG helicase gene in many bacteria, including pathogenic Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Pseudomonas putida. Analysis of P. putida transcriptomes shows that many OxyR-controlled genes are regulated by the ATP-dependent RecG helicase and that RecG alone modulates the expression of many genes. We found that purified RecG binds to the promoters of many OxyR-controlled genes and that expression of these genes was not induced under conditions of oxidative stress in recG mutants of P. aeruginosa, P. putida, and Escherichia coli. In vitro data revealed that promoters containing palindromic sequences are essential for RecG binding and that single-strand binding proteins and ATP are also needed for RecG to promote transcription, whereas a magnesium ion has the opposite effect. The OxyR tetramer preferentially binds to promoters after RecG has generated linear DNA in the presence of ATP; otherwise, the OxyR dimer has higher affinity. This study provides new insights into the mechanism of bacterial transcription by demonstrating that RecG might be required for the induction of the OxyR regulon by unwinding palindromic DNA for transcription. This work describes a novel bacterial transcriptional function by RecG helicase with OxyR and may provide new targets for controlling Pseudomonas species pathogen.

Abstract:
The biological role of REEs has long been underestimated, and research has mainly focused on methanotrophic and methylotrophic bacteria. We have recently demonstrated that P. putida, a plant growth-promoting bacterium that thrives in the rhizosphere of various food crops, possesses a REE-dependent alcohol dehydrogenase (PedH), but knowledge about REE-specific effects on physiological traits in nonmethylotrophic bacteria is still scarce. This study demonstrates that the cellular response of P. putida to lanthanum (La3+) is mostly substrate specific and that La3+ availability highly affects the growth of cells on glycerol. Further, a novel route for glycerol metabolism is identified, which is initiated by PedE and/or PedH activity and provides a growth advantage to this biotechnologically relevant organism by allowing a faster onset of growth. Overall, these findings demonstrate that lanthanides can affect physiological traits in nonmethylotrophic bacteria and might influence their competitiveness in various environmental niches. ABSTRACT Ever since the discovery of the first rare earth element (REE)-dependent enzyme, the physiological role of lanthanides has become an emerging field of research due to the environmental implications and biotechnological opportunities. In Pseudomonas putida KT2440, the two pyrroloquinoline quinone-dependent alcohol dehydrogenases (PQQ-ADHs) PedE and PedH are inversely regulated in response to REE availability. This transcriptional switch is orchestrated by a complex regulatory network that includes the PedR2/PedS2 two-component system and is important for efficient growth on several alcoholic volatiles. To study whether cellular responses beyond the REE switch exist, the differential proteomic responses that occur during growth on various model carbon sources were analyzed. Apart from the Ca2+-dependent enzyme PedE, the differential abundances of most identified proteins were conditional. During growth on glycerol—and concomitant with the proteomic changes—lanthanum (La3+) availability affected different growth parameters, including the onset of logarithmic growth and final optical densities. Studies with mutant strains revealed a novel metabolic route for glycerol utilization, initiated by PedE and/or PedH activity. Upon oxidation to glycerate via glyceraldehyde, phosphorylation by the glycerate kinase GarK most likely yields glycerate-2-phosphate, which is eventually channeled into the central metabolism of the cell. This new route functions in parallel with the main degradation pathway encoded by the glpFKRD operon and provides a growth advantage to the cells by allowing an earlier onset of growth with glycerol as the sole source of carbon and energy. IMPORTANCE The biological role of REEs has long been underestimated, and research has mainly focused on methanotrophic and methylotrophic bacteria. We have recently demonstrated that P. putida, a plant growth-promoting bacterium that thrives in the rhizosphere of various food crops, possesses a REE-dependent alcohol dehydrogenase (PedH), but knowledge about REE-specific effects on physiological traits in nonmethylotrophic bacteria is still scarce. This study demonstrates that the cellular response of P. putida to lanthanum (La3+) is mostly substrate specific and that La3+ availability highly affects the growth of cells on glycerol. Further, a novel route for glycerol metabolism is identified, which is initiated by PedE and/or PedH activity and provides a growth advantage to this biotechnologically relevant organism by allowing a faster onset of growth. Overall, these findings demonstrate that lanthanides can affect physiological traits in nonmethylotrophic bacteria and might influence their competitiveness in various environmental niches.

Abstract:
Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a Gram-negative environmental and human opportunistic pathogen highly adapted to many different environmental conditions. It can cause a wide range of serious infections, including wounds, lungs, the urinary tract, and systemic infections. The high versatility and pathogenicity of this bacterium is attributed to its genomic complexity, the expression of several virulence factors, and its intrinsic resistance to various antimicrobials. However, to thrive and establish infection, P. aeruginosa must overcome several barriers. One of these barriers is the presence of oxidizing agents (e.g., hydrogen peroxide, superoxide, and hypochlorous acid) produced by the host immune system or that are commonly used as disinfectants in a variety of different environments including hospitals. These agents damage several cellular molecules and can cause cell death. Therefore, bacteria adapt to these harsh conditions by altering gene expression and eliciting several stress responses to survive under oxidative stress. Here, we used PubMed to evaluate the current knowledge on the oxidative stress responses adopted by P. aeruginosa. We will describe the genes that are often differently expressed under oxidative stress conditions, the pathways and proteins employed to sense and respond to oxidative stress, and how these changes in gene expression influence pathogenicity and the virulence of P. aeruginosa. Understanding these responses and changes in gene expression is critical to controlling bacterial pathogenicity and developing new therapeutic agents.

Abstract:
Alkyl hydroperoxide reductase (Ahp), comprised of four different subunits AhpC, AhpD, AhpE, and AhpF, is a thiol-based antioxidative enzyme with the ability to protect bacteria against oxidative stress. Functionally, AhpC and AhpE considered as peroxidases directly detoxify peroxides, while AhpD and AhpF as oxidoreductases restore oxidized peroxidases to their reduced form. Corynebacterium glutamicum ncgl0877 encodes a putative Ahp with a unique Cys-Pro-Phe-Cys (C-P-G-C) active-site motif, similar with those of the thiol-disulfide oxidoreductases such as thioredoxin (Trx), mycoredoxin-1 (Mrx1) and AhpD. However, its physiological and biochemical functions remain unknown in C. glutamicum. Here, we report that NCgl0877, designated CgAhp, is involved in the protection against organic peroxide (OP) stress. The cgahp-deleted strain is notably more sensitive to OP stress. The cgahp expression is controlled by a MarR-type transcriptional repressor OasR (organic peroxide- and antibiotic-sensing regulator). The physiological role of CgAhp in resistance to OP stresses is corroborated by its induced expression under stresses. Although CgAhp has a weak peroxidase activity toward OP, it mainly supports the OP-scavenging activity of the thiol-dependent peroxidase preferentially linked to the dihydrolipoamide dehydrogenase (Lpd)/dihydrolipoamide succinyltransferase (SucB)/NADH system. The C-P-G-C motif of CgAhp is essential to maintain the reductase activity. In conclusion, our study identifies CgAhp, behaving like AhpD, as a key disulfide oxidoreductase involved in the oxidative stress tolerance and the functional electron donor for peroxidase.

Abstract:
<jats:p>Multiple drug resistance (MDR) bacterial strains are responsible by 1.2 million of human deaths all over the world. The pathogens possess efficient enzymes which are able to mitigate the toxicity of reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced by some antibiotics and the host immune cells. Among them, the bacterial peroxiredoxin alkyl hydroperoxide reductase C (AhpC) is able to decompose efficiently several kinds of hydroperoxides. To decompose their substrates AhpC use a reactive cysteine residue (peroxidatic cysteine—Cys<jats:sub>P</jats:sub>) that together with two other polar residues (Thr/Ser and Arg) comprise the catalytic triad of these enzymes and are involved in the substrate targeting/stabilization to allow a bimolecular nucleophilic substitution (S<jats:sub>N</jats:sub>2) reaction. Additionally to the high efficiency the AhpC is very abundant in the cells and present virulent properties in some bacterial species. Despite the importance of AhpC in bacteria, few studies aimed at using natural compounds as inhibitors of this class of enzymes. Some natural products were identified as human isoforms, presenting as common characteristics a bulk hydrophobic moiety and an α, β-unsaturated carbonylic system able to perform a thiol-Michael reaction. In this work, we evaluated two chemically related natural products: 1,4-dihydroxy-2-(3’,7’-dimethyl-1’-oxo-2’<jats:italic>E</jats:italic>,6’-octadienyl) benzene (<jats:bold>C1</jats:bold>) and 4-hydroxy-2-(3’,7’-dimethyl-1’-oxo-2’<jats:italic>E</jats:italic>,6’-octadienyl) benzoic acid (<jats:bold>C2</jats:bold>), both were isolated from branches<jats:italic>Piper crassinervium</jats:italic>(Piperaceae), over the peroxidase activity of AhpC from<jats:italic>Pseudomonas aeruginosa</jats:italic>(PaAhpC) and<jats:italic>Staphylococcus epidermidis</jats:italic>(SeAhpC). By biochemical assays we show that although both compounds can perform the Michael addition reaction, only compound<jats:bold>C2</jats:bold>was able to inhibit the PaAhpC peroxidase activity but not SeAhpC, presenting IC<jats:sub>50</jats:sub>= 20.3 μM. SDS-PAGE analysis revealed that the compound was not able to perform a thiol-Michael addition, suggesting another inhibition behavior. Using computer-assisted simulations, we also show that an acidic group present in the structure of compound<jats:bold>C2</jats:bold>may be involved in the stabilization by polar interactions with the Thr and Arg residues from the catalytic triad and several apolar interactions with hydrophobic residues. Finally,<jats:bold>C2</jats:bold>was not able to interfere in the peroxidase activity of the isoform Prx2 from humans or even the thiol proteins of the Trx reducing system from<jats:italic>Escherichia coli</jats:italic>(EcTrx and EcTrxR), indicating specificity for<jats:italic>P</jats:italic>.<jats:italic>aeruginosa</jats:italic>AhpC.</jats:p>

## 3. Comparative Genomic and Transcriptomic Analysis of Phenol Degradation and Tolerance in Acinetobacter lwoffii through Adaptive Evolution

Abstract:
Microorganism-based methods have been widely applied for the treatment of phenol-polluted environments. The previously isolated Acinetobacter lwoffii NL1 strain could completely degrade 0.5 g/L phenol within 12 h, but not higher concentrations of phenol. In this study, we developed an evolutionary strain NL115, through adaptive laboratory evolution, which possessed improved degradation ability and was able to degrade 1.5 g/L phenol within 12 h. Compared with that of the starting strain NL1, the concentration of degradable phenol by the developed strain increased three-fold; its phenol tolerance was also enhanced. Furthermore, comparative genomics showed that sense mutations mainly occurred in genes encoding alkyl hydroperoxide reductase, phenol hydroxylase, 30S ribosomal protein, and mercury resistance operon. Comparative transcriptomics between A. lwoffii NL115 and NL1 revealed the enrichment of direct degradation, stress resistance, and vital activity processes among the metabolic responses of A. lwoffii adapted to phenol stress. Among these, all the upregulated genes (log2fold-change > 5) encoded peroxidases. A phenotypic comparison of A. lwoffii NL1 and NL115 found that the adapted strain NL115 exhibited strengthened antioxidant capacity. Furthermore, the increased enzymatic activities of phenol hydroxylase and alkyl hydroperoxide reductase in A. lwoffii NL115 validated their response to phenol. Overall, this study provides insight into the mechanism of efficient phenol degradation through adaptive microbial evolution and can help to drive improvements in phenol bioremediation.

    issn = "2045-2322"
